---
title: "coordinates"
subtitle: "Grids, point sets, projection and interpolation in Fortran"
---

The **coordinates** library manages grids and arbitrary sets of points, and
maps fields between them. It handles projection between coordinate systems,
nearest-neighbor and conservative interpolation, and caches the (expensive)
mapping calculation to disk so it is only ever done once per grid pair.

It is the workhorse behind regional/global climate and ice-sheet model
pre-processing: taking observations or model output on one grid (a global
lat-lon grid, an unstructured set of station locations, polygon data) and
remapping them onto another (e.g. a regional oblique-stereographic grid over
Greenland or Antarctica).

## The mental model

Everything is built on **points**. A `grid` is just a structured 2D
arrangement of points, and a `map` records, for each target point, which
source points feed it and with what weight. Because the design pushes all the
state into these three objects, mapping a field between two domains takes only
three steps and no temporary bookkeeping variables.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A["grid_class / points_class<br/>(source)"] -->|map_init| M["map_class<br/>(neighbors + weights,<br/>cached to NetCDF)"]
    B["grid_class / points_class<br/>(target)"] -->|map_init| M
    M -->|map_field| F["field on target grid"]
    V["field on source grid"] --> F
```

## The three-step example

```fortran
use coord

type(grid_class) :: gCCSM3, gGRL
type(map_class)  :: map1

! 1. Define each grid (global lat-lon source, regional stereographic target)
call grid_init(gCCSM3, name="CCSM3-T42", mtype="latlon", units="degrees", &
               x0=0.d0,   dx=2.d0, nx=180, y0=-90.d0, dy=2.d0, ny=90)

call grid_init(gGRL, name="GRL-20KM", mtype="stereographic", units="kilometers", &
               dx=20.d0, nx=76, dy=20.d0, ny=151, &
               lambda=-40.d0, phi=72.d0, alpha=7.5d0)

! 2. Build (or load from cache) the mapping between them
call map_init(map1, gCCSM3, gGRL, max_neighbors=10)

! 3. Map a field
call map_field(map1, "t2m", var_ccsm3, var_grl, mask_map, method="quadrant")
```

The first `map_init` for a given grid pair computes neighbors and writes a
NetCDF map file under `maps/`. Every later run loads that file instead of
recomputing — so the up-front cost (often >1 minute on large grids) is paid
once.

## What's documented here

- **[Concepts](concepts.qmd)** — the points / grid / map data model and the
  philosophy of points as the primitive.
- **API** — the public interface, organised by module:
  [coordinates](api/coordinates.qmd) (building points & grids),
  [mapping](api/mapping.qmd) (`map_init` / `map_field`),
  [conservative](api/conservative.qmd) remapping,
  the [SCRIP/CDO](api/scrip-cdo.qmd) path, and
  [helper modules](api/helpers.qmd).
- **Reference** — [supported projections & planets](reference/projections.qmd)
  and the [on-disk map file formats](reference/map-file-format.qmd).
- **[Improvements](improvements.qmd)** — a focused design proposal for replacing
  the brute-force neighbor search with a KD-tree and the subgrid-sampling
  conservative weights with analytic spherical-polygon clipping, removing the
  external CDO dependency while keeping `map_init` / `map_field` unchanged.

## Installation in brief

The library depends only on a Fortran compiler and **NetCDF** (the bundled
[NCIO](https://github.com/alex-robinson/ncio) wraps it). The optional
conservative-remapping-via-CDO path additionally calls the `cdo` command-line
tool at runtime. Build the static library with `make coordinates` and link
against `libcoordinates/include/libcoordinates.a`, then `use coord` in your
program.

::: {.callout-note}
This documentation was reconstructed by reading the source. Line references
(e.g. `coordinates_mapping.f90:450`) point at the version of the code in this
repository and are meant as navigation aids.
:::
